narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 43, which stands out above the typical student's score. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 66% chance that you would not pass, leading to the difficult decision to reject your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from Years 1 and 3, both at 3, which are below the averages of around 5.7 and 5.5 for most students in the dataset—these lower rankings had the strongest pull toward forecasting a bar failure. Your LSAT, on the other hand, worked strongly in your favor, pushing the prediction toward success and nearly offsetting those early academic signals. Other factors, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 (slightly below the average of 3.21) and middle family income bracket, also nudged the outlook toward failure, though less forcefully, while your choice of full-time study had minimal impact. Compared to typical applicants, where about 93% plan full-time enrollment like you, your profile's early-year performance lags behind the stronger starts that often correlate with bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model prioritizes these patterns from past students to minimize risks for the program. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted LSAT maintenance or GPA-enhancing opportunities to build on your strengths. Reach out to advisors for personalized prep strategies tailored to your background. We're rooting for you to reapply stronger—your journey is far from over. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
